coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of surface-group-tunable red emissive carbon dots and their applications for Fe3+ and pyrophosphate detection
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiang
Yan, Xiu
Wang, Conghui
Ma, Yunxiao
Jiang, Yi
Wang, Rongjing
Shi, Dongyang
Li, Zhongjian
Zhu, Guifen
Tan, Bing
coredata.dc:description: Red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) have great potential applications in optical and electronic devices, but few studies have focused on synthesizing R-CDs with tunable functional groups. In this study, p-phenylenediamine and hydroquinone were selected as precursors with specific hybridized carbon structures for synthesizing R-CDs with tunable functional groups by hydrothermal method. The proportion of precursors in the reaction system were adjusted to successfully synthesize R-CDs with various contents of oxygen and amino groups. Experimental results showed that the fluorescence of the R-CDs was effectively quenched by Fe3+ through the formation of R-CDs/Fe3+ complex. The fluorescence quenching ability of Fe3+ for R-CDs with different N/O ratios were also investigated. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of Fe3+ for R-CDs increased with the oxygen content of the R-CDs. By selecting R-CDs with oxygen-containing surface groups (R-O-CDs) as nanoprobe, an “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor has been developed for dual-detection of Fe3+ and pyrophosphate (PPi). A detection limit of 0.527 μM for Fe3+ and 0.24 μM for PPi were achieved. The as-developed R-O-CDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe was also successfully used to detect Fe3+ and PPi in real samples. Furthermore, an IMPLICATION logic gate was designed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emissive carbon dots
Tunable functional groups
Fe3+
Pyrophosphate
IMPLICATION logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of Fe3+ and PPi in buffer solution
Fluorescent detection of PPi in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of the prepared CDs
Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
Sensitivity of the sensing system for PPi detection
Selectivity of the sensing system for PPi detection
Fluorescence detection of PPi in synovial fluids
Construction of a fluorescent IMPLICATION logic gate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS full spectra (a) of B-CDs, R-CDs and R-O-CDs; The high-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectrum of B-CDs (b, c), R-CDs (d, e, f) and R-O-CDs (g, h, i).
PL excitation (blue line) and emission spectra (red line) of B-CDs (a), R-O-CDs (b) and R-CDs (c), respectively. The inset displays photographs of the CDs under UV irradiation in ethanol solution.

(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of R-O-CDs. The inset displays the size distribution of R-O-CDs; (c) XRD and (d) Raman spectra of R-O-CDs; (e) Synthesis of R-O-CDs and the detection mechanism for Fe3+ and PPi.

(a). Selectivity of R-O-CDs for Fe3+ over other metal ions (gray bars) and the mixture of Fe3+ with other metal ions (black bars). (b) Fluorescence spectra of R-O-CDs in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.0, 1 mM NaCl) upon addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0–500 μM) at the optimum excitation wavelength of 550 nm; (c) FL intensity change (F-F0)/F0 of the resultant mixture vs Fe3+ concentration. Inset: the linear relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and Fe3+ concentration; (d) The UV–vis absorption spectrum (black curve) of Fe3+, and the excitation (red curve) and emission spectra (blue curve) of R-O-CDs; (e) The scheme of PET mechanism between R-O-CDs and Fe3+; (f) Zeta potentials of the R-O-CDs, Fe3+, R-O-CDs + Fe3+ and R-O-CDs + Fe3++ PPi under the same condition.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-O-CDs in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.0, 1 mM NaCl) in the presence of different composition. Curve a: R-O-CDs only; curve b: R-O-CDs/Fe3+, curve c: R-O-CDs/Fe3++PPi; curve d: R-O-CDs + PPi; (b) Fluorescence response of the R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex system in Tris-HCl buffer solution after incubation with various concentrations of PPi under the optimum excitation wavelength; (c) Plot of F/F0 vs the PPi concentration. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex system in the absence and presence of PPi, respectively; (d) Selectivity of R-O-CDs/Fe3+ for PPi over other metal ions (black bars) and the mixture of PPi with other metal ions (red bars).

(a) Truth table of the IMPLICATION logic gate; (b) the symbol for the IMPLICATION logic gate; (c) Diagram of the output signals under the different inputs; (d) reversible switching of the IMPLICATION logic gate.
Results of Fe3+ detection in real water samples.
Results of PPi detection in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Samples 1, 2 and 3 were obtained from arthritis patients; samples 4, 5 and 6 were obtained from healthy volunteers.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each sample was analyzed in triplicate, and the results were averaged.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of surface-group-tunable red emissive carbon dots and their applications for Fe3+ and pyrophosphate detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environment and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xingyang People's Hospital, Xingyang, Henan 450100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xingyang People's Hospital, Xingyang, Henan 450100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) have great potential applications in optical and electronic devices, but few studies have focused on synthesizing R-CDs with tunable functional groups. In this study, p-phenylenediamine and hydroquinone were selected as precursors with specific hybridized carbon structures for synthesizing R-CDs with tunable functional groups by hydrothermal method. The proportion of precursors in the reaction system were adjusted to successfully synthesize R-CDs with various contents of oxygen and amino groups. Experimental results showed that the fluorescence of the R-CDs was effectively quenched by Fe3+ through the formation of R-CDs/Fe3+ complex. The fluorescence quenching ability of Fe3+ for R-CDs with different N/O ratios were also investigated. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of Fe3+ for R-CDs increased with the oxygen content of the R-CDs. By selecting R-CDs with oxygen-containing surface groups (R-O-CDs) as nanoprobe, an “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor has been developed for dual-detection of Fe3+ and pyrophosphate (PPi). A detection limit of 0.527 μM for Fe3+ and 0.24 μM for PPi were achieved. The as-developed R-O-CDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe was also successfully used to detect Fe3+ and PPi in real samples. Furthermore, an IMPLICATION logic gate was designed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a necessary metal ion in living systems, Fe3+ plays an important role in many physiological processes such as oxygen transport, DNA and RNA synthesis, enzyme catalysis and cellular metabolism [1,2]. Abnormal level of Fe3+ in human body would cause a serious of diseases, such as intelligence decline, arthritis, anemia, diabetes, heart failure, and cancer [3]. On the other hand, pyrophosphate (PPi) is a primary biological anion, which is a by-product of adenosine hydrolysis and enzymatic reaction. It involves many physiological processes, such as DNA replication, signal transduction, protein synthesis, calcium release from mitochondrial stores, energy storage and so on [3]. Once the level of PPi is abnormal, it leads to hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, chondrocalcinosis, and dehydrated calcium pyrophosphate crystal deposition [4]. Thus, developing the high sensitivity and selectivity method for Fe3+ and PPi determination is great importance in the area of early identification and diagnosis of diseases.

Carbon dots (CDs) are representative carbon-based zero-dimensional structures that have attracted considerable attention in recent years [5]. CDs have the advantages of low cost, good water solubility, multicolor emission and tunable surface functionalities [6]. More importantly, CDs are a novel fluorescent nanomaterial with a preparation process that does not involve the use of heavy metals and is therefore environmentally friendly. As a result, rapid progress has been made in the use of CDs for Fe3+ and ppi fluorescent sensors [6,7]. The use of CDs for fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and ppi has increased considerably, but a continuing problem is that most reported CDs exhibit blue or green fluorescent emission under ultraviolet excitation. Some proteins or small biological molecules also exhibit blue autofluorescence under ultraviolet excitation in a complex biological matrix [8,9]. Thus, the detection signal produced by blue luminous CDs is influenced by the autofluorescence of the biological matrix, which makes it difficult to discriminate the detection signal from the background signal. Therefore, the preparation of CDs with long-wavelength emissions and applying them in the field of biosensing are particularly important. Recently, the preparation of CDs with red emission (R-CDs) has increasingly attracted considerable attention [10–12]. Theoretical predictions and experimental results have led to the claim that the preparation of R-CDs can be facilitated by using high contents of conjugated sp2-hybridized carbon and N-dopants in the reaction system [13–15]. Aniline analogs (such as o-phenylenediamine, p-phenylenediamine, 4-aminophenol and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene, which contain sp2 hybridized structures) have been used as precursors to synthesize R-CDs by hydrothermal methods [16–18]. However, these reported R-CDs have monotonic surface functional groups consisting of amino groups and a few oxygen groups, which were not suitable as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions. In order to obtain R-CDs with oxygen-containing surface groups (R-O-CDs), concentrated sulfuric acid was used as catalyst and dehydrating agent to oxidize CDs [19–20]. However, as strong acids are very dangerous to use, it is particularly important to develop a relatively mild preparation method to prepare R-CDs with controllable contents of surface amino and oxygen groups.

Herein, we synthesized R-CDs for which the functional groups could be tuned by changing the hybridized structure of the precursors. We selected hydroquinone and p-phenylenediamine as the precursors. R-CDs with various contents of oxygen and amino groups were successfully synthesized under mild solvothermal conditions by simply adjusting the proportions of the precursors. The fluorescence quenching ability of Fe3+ for R-CDs with different N/O ratios were investigated. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of Fe3+ for the R-CDs increased with the oxygen content of the R-CDs. Subsequently, pyrophosphate (PPi) was added to the R-O-CDs/Fe3+ system, and the complexation of PPi with Fe3+ resulted in the release of Fe3+ from the surface of the R-O-CDs, accompanied by the fluorescence recovered. Then, an “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor has been developed for dual-detection of Fe3+ and PPi. Under the optimized conditions, sensitive and specific quantifications of Fe3+ and PPi were achieved accordingly.

Hydroquinone, p-phenylenediamine, and sodium pyrophosphate were purchased from the Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the water used in the experiment was distilled, 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution (1 mM NaCl, pH = 7.0) was used for the detection of PPi, and all the reagents were analytical grade and were used without further treatment.

A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) was used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the R-CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired on an X'Pert3 powder X-ray diffractometer.

(PANalytical B.V.). Raman spectra were measured using an inVia photoluminescence-laser Raman spectrometer (Beijing Jin Xianfeng Technology Co., Ltd., China) with a 633 nm laser. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out using a Spectrum 400F spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Massachusetts, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-T6 spectrophotometer (Purkinie, Beijing, China). Zeta potentials were measured by using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 dynamic light-scattering system (Malvern Instruments Co, Ltd). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Varian, California, USA).

10 mg of p-phenylenediamine and 10 mg of hydroquinone were dissolved in 25 mL of ethanol. The mixture solution was transferred to a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and allowed at react at 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, the supernatant and residue were separated by centrifugation at a speed of 10000 rpm for 10 min, and the solvent was removed by rotary evaporation. The crude products were further purified using dichloromethane and methanol (10:1, V: V) as eluents and a column layer silica gel column to obtain a dark red solid powder to which a prescribed quantity of deionized water was added. The resulting aqueous suspension of R-O-CDs was stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Fe3+ and PPi in buffer solution
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical Fe3+ detection, the freshly prepared ferric chloride solution with different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 200, 350, 350 and 500 μM) were introduced into 2 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0, 1 mM NaCl) containing 50 μL of R-O-CDs for 10 min at 25 °C. The fluorescence spectrum were recorded under excitation at 550 nm.

For the determination of PPi, the following procedure was carried out. Different concentrations of PPi were dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer containing 50 μL of R-O-CDs and 150 μM Fe3+. The final mixture volume was also maintained at 2 mL and was incubated for 10 min at 25 °C, then the fluorescence intensity was measured at λex = 550 nm. Blank experiments were carried out using the same procedure for the purpose of comparison. To investigate the selectivity of the sensor system, interfering compounds at different concentrations were incubated with the sensing solution at 25 °C for 10 min. The same procedural steps were carried out as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of PPi in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate PPi detection in real biological samples, synovial fluid samples were collected from three arthritis patients and three healthy volunteers at Xingyang People’s Hospital. Briefly, the six synovial fluid samples were centrifuged at 1000 × g for 20 min using an ultrafiltration device (molecular weight cutoff 50 kDa; Millipore Amicon Ultra). The samples were diluted with the buffer solution and subjected to fluorescent detection of PPi. The same process was used to detect PPi in the synovial fluid samples as for the buffer solution. The research study was approved by the Xingyang People’s Hospital Ethics Committee, and all participants provided written informed consent before the samples were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: p-phenylenediamine was selected as an excellent precursor for the synthesis of R-CDs [21]. In order to obtain R-CDs with oxygen-containing surface groups (R-O-CDs), different oxygenated chemical substances (such as citric acid, fumaric acid, sucrose, malic acid, tartaric acid, ascorbic acid and oxalic acid) were selected to react with p-phenylenediamine using a mild one-pot hydrothermal method. Most of the reaction solutions emitted blue or green fluorescence (as shown in Fig. S1). This phenomenon showed that the above mentioned substances, which all contain sp3 hybridized carbon atom structures, could not be used to synthesize R-CDs by the mild one-pot hydrothermal method. However, R-O-CDs could be synthesized by mixing hydroquinone and p-phenylenediamine via the mild one-pot hydrothermal method. Increasing the p-phenylenediamine content caused the emission of CDs to gradually change from blue to red light (as shown in Fig. S2). It is speculated that hydroquinone, which possesses a benzene ring with a sp2 hybridized carbon, participated in a Schiff base reaction with p-phenylenediamine to form large conjugated sp2 domains [22].

Control experiments were performed to prove that the R-O-CDs were produced by the interaction of p-phenylenediamine and hydroquinone. Fig. S3 shows that using only hydroquinone or p-phenylenediamine as a precursor yielded blue (B-CDs) or red (R-CDs) fluorescent CDs. XPS was used to determine the chemical states and compositions of the three CDs. The full-range XPS spectrum clearly showed that the R-CDs and R-O-CDs were composed of C, N, and O elements, and the B-CDs were composed of C and O elements (Fig. 1 a). The C, N and O contents of the B-CDs, R-CDs and R-O-CDs are listed in Table S1. The elemental N content of the R-O-CDs was significantly higher than that of the B-CDs. The elemental O content of the R-O-CDs was higher than that of the R-CDs. Fig. 1b, d and g show that the high-resolution C1s spectra exhibited several peaks. The spectral peaks for the B-CDs correspond to three chemical bonds, CC/CC (284.6 eV), CO (286.1 eV) and CO (288.1 eV); the C1s spectrum of the R-CDs exhibited two peaks: CC/CC (284.7 eV) and CN/CO (286 eV); and the spectrum of the R-O-CDs exhibited four peaks: CC/CC (284.7 eV), CN/CN (285.6 eV), CO (286.5 eV) and O-CO/N-CO (288.9 eV) [23,24]. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 1e and h: the R-CDs and R-O-CDs contained pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N with binding energies of 398.2 eV and 399.4 eV; 400.1 eV and 398.7 eV; and 399.3 eV and 400.2 eV, respectively. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectra of the three CDs is shown in Fig. 1c, f, and i, where each spectrum exhibits two peaks: CO (530.3 eV) and COC (532.1 eV); COC (531.2 eV) and CO (532.9 eV); and CO (532.3 eV) and OC-O (533.5 eV) [24,25].

The proportions of the components of the three CDs are listed in Table S2. More carboxyl groups were introduced into the R-O-CDs than the R-CDs. To determine the CD surface chemical composition, FT-IR spectra were obtained for the three CDs. Fig. S4a shows that the spectrum for the R-O-CDs exhibited three characteristic peaks that were not present in the spectrum of the B-CDs, i.e., NH (3321 cm−1), CN (1630 cm−1) and CN (1365 cm−1), indicating that the R-O-CDs had been successfully doped with N atoms. In addition, the spectrum of the R-O-CDs contained a minor peak at 1711 cm−1 that was not present in the spectrum of the R-CDs and related to CO stretching, suggesting that the R-O-CD surface contained carboxyl groups, which was consistent with the XPS spectra. Comparison with the FT-IR spectra of hydroquinone in the literature, the OH stretching vibration peaks at 3260 cm−1 for the R-O-CDs were significantly weaker and contained a new absorption band at 1630 cm−1 that was assigned as a CN stretching vibration [26]. Therefore, the synthesis of R-O-CDs was attributed to a Schiff base reaction, in which hydroquinone was first oxidized to 1,4-benzoquinone, followed by Schiff condensation between hydroquinone and p-phenylenediamine in ethanol [26,27]. The FTIR and XPS spectra all indicated abundant amino (–NH2), carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups on the surface of the as-prepared R-O-CDs, which improve the water solubility and stability of R-O-CDs in aqueous solution [18,28–30]. The UV spectra of the three CDs were also different. Fig. S4b shows that in the high-energy UV regime, the spectrum of the B-CDs contained two prominent peaks at 210 nm and 276 nm, which correspond to π–π* transitions of localized sp2 clusters in aromatic CC bonds and n–π* transitions of the CO bands of carboxyl groups on the surface of the B-CDs [31]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the R-O-CDs exhibited a broad absorption band centered at 575 nm, which was assigned to the n–π* transition of the surface/molecular section, resulting in strong fluorescence emission with two absorption bands at approximately 223 nm and 287 nm that reflected the π-π* transition of the CC bonds and the n-π* and π-π* transitions in the aromatic sp2 system containing CO and CN bonds, respectively. In the spectra of R-CDs, the intense peaks at 215 nm, 243 nm and 278 nm were ascribed to π-π* transitions of the aromatic CC bonds and the broad peak at 463 nm was ascribed to the n-π* transitions of the CN bonds [17,18]. By comparison, the absorption peak in the spectrum of the R-O-CDs was red-shifted, which was inferred to correspond to the formation of a small electron band gap during the polymerization and carbonization process.

The fluorescence excitation and emission of the three CDs were also tested. As shown in Fig. 2 , Three CDs show different excitation and emission wavelengths. The fluorescence spectra of three CDs were also measured at different excitation wavelengths. Fig. S5 shows that when the excitation wavelength increased from 500 nm to 575 nm, there was almost no shift in the maximum emission peak in the spectrum of the R-O-CDs, which indicated excitation-independent fluorescent behavior for the as-prepared R-O-CDs. By contrast, the B-CDs exhibited clear excitation-dependent behavior, whereas the behavior of the R-CDs was weakly excitation-dependent.

TEM was used to investigate the microstructure of the as-prepared R-O-CDs. The as-prepared R-O-CDs consisted of uniform spherical nanoparticles with good monodispersity and average sizes of approximately 3.5 ± 1.2 nm (Fig. 3 a). Fig. 3b shows there were prominent lattice fringes in the high-resolution TEM micrographs of the R-O-CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm that was attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphene. The XRD pattern of the R-O-CDs (Fig. 3c) showed a sharp strong peak at 2θ = 21°, indicating a graphene structure that was consistent with the HRTEM results. Fig. 3d shows the Raman spectrum of R-O-CDs, in which the D band (1347 cm−1) and G band (1567 cm−1) were related to the presence of sp2 and sp3 carbons and the intensity ratio between I D and I G was 0.82 [32].

We used rhodamine B (QY = 56 %, in ethanol) as a reference to measure the QY of R-O-CDs in ethanol as 24.6 % under 550 nm excitation. The stability of the R-O-CDs was also investigated under different conditions. The prepared R-O-CDs had good photobleaching resistance, and the fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged after 15 days of visible light irradiation (Fig. S6a). The fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs under different salt concentrations was also measured. Fig. S6b shows that the fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs was very stable even under a high salt concentration (150 mM NaCl). We next investigated the fluorescence behavior of the R-O-CDs under different pH conditions. Fig. S7 shows that the fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs gradually increased as the pH increased from 3.0 to 7.0 and reached a maximum in the pH range of 7.0–10.0. At pH greater than 10.0, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly. This result indicated that the prepared R-O-CDs had excellent fluorescence properties under physiological pH conditions (pH∼7.0) and therefore considerable application potential for biomolecular detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: We also investigate the fluorescence quenching ability of different metal ions (such as Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Sr2+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, and Ag+) for the prepared R-O-CDs. Fig. 4 a shows that the presence of Fe3+ induced a dramatic decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the prepared R-O-CDs, whereas other metal ions had little or no influence on the fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs. Fluorescence emission spectra were measured for the R-O-CDs with added Fe3+ at different concentrations (0–500 μM). Fig. 4b shows that the fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs gradually decreased with increasing Fe3+ concentration. The calibration curve between the fluorescence intensity and the Fe3+ concentration is shown in Fig. 4c. A good linear relationship is obtained over the range 1–80 μM (inset in Fig. 4c). This equation can be expressed as (F0-F)/F0 = 0.00373 × CFe 3+ (μM) + 0.00994 with R2 = 0.998. The detection limit (LOD) of Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.527 μM (S/N = 3). As compared with other CDs-based fluorescence sensors for Fe3+ detection (Table S3), our sensor shows a lower detection limit than most of the previously reported CDs-based fluorescence sensors for Fe3+. The fluorescence quenching ability of Fe3+ for CDs with different N/O ratios was also investigated. Table S4 shows significant differences among the fluorescence quenching effects of Fe3+ for CDs with different N/O ratios. These results showed that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Fe3+ to CDs increased with the oxygen content of the CDs. The high efficiency of Fe3+ in quenching the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was attributed to the unique coordination between the phenolic hydroxyl group and Fe3+ [33]. Fe3+ exhibited a considerably higher quenching ability for R-O-CDs than R-CDs. This result indicated that introducing hydroquinone into the R-O-CD preparation materials increased the content of the phenol hydroxyl group and thereby improved the sensitivity. We speculated there were two reasons for this behavior: one reason was that after hydroquinone was oxidized to p-benzoquinone, the Schiff base reacted with p-phenylenediamine to enhance the complexing ability of Fe3+; the other reason was that the complexing ability of R-O-CDs with both –OH and –NH2 on the surface was superior to that with –NH2 alone on the surface. Therefore, the R-O-CDs are more suitable as fluorescence “off” sensors for Fe3+ determination than R-CDs.

To elucidate the mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of R-O-CDs by Fe3+, the Lineweaver-Burk equation ((1/(F0-F) = 1/F0 + KLB/(F0*Cq)) were used to fit the fluorescence quenching data, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the R-O-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively; Cq denotes the concentration of the quencher (Fe3+); KLB is the static quenching constant [34]. Fig. S8 shows a good linear correlation between the Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence intensity using the Lineweaver-Burk equation: 1/(F0-F) = 0.6097 × 1/CFe 3+ (μM−1) −0.00341(R2 = 0.9989). Based on these results, it was speculated that Fe3+ binds with carboxyl and organic nitrogen groups on the surface of R-O-CDs to form an R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex, resulting in static fluorescence quenching [32,35]. To further explore fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of R-O-CDs in the absence or presence of Fe3 + ions were measured. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence lifetime of R-O-CDs and R-O-CDs/Fe3+ is 1.67 ns and 1.82 ns, respectively. There are no obvious changes in the fluorescence lifetime, confirming the static quenching mechanism of R-O-CDs caused by Fe3 + due to the formation of R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complexs. Fig. S10 shows that the fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs was quenched instantly and reached equilibrium within 3 min.

To further explore the quenching mechanism, we studied the optical properties of Fe3+ and R-O-CDs. Fig. 4d shows there was little overlap between the excitation spectrum of the R-O-CDs and the absorption spectrum of Fe3+. Therefore, an internal filtration effect was ruled out for this system. Fig. S11a shows the electronic bandgap (Eg) of the R-O-CDs obtained by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. The Tauc plot shows that the direct electronic bandgap of the R-O-CDs was approximately 1.96 eV. The valence band (VB) of the R-O-CDs was determined to be 2.24 eV by XPS (Fig. S11b). Thus, the conduction band (CB) of the R-O-CDs was calculated to be 0.28 eV from the difference between Eg and VB. The redox potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ is 0.77 V vs NHE, which is located between the CB and VB of the R-O-CDs. For this reason, the fluorescence quenching process of R-O-CDs by Fe3+ conformed to a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. Fig. 4e shows that the electrons in the VB of the R-O-CDs under light excitation were excited to the CB, and the excited electrons were then transferred to the d orbit of Fe, whereby the fluorescence of the R-O-CDs was quenched via the PET process. This mechanism is similar to the electronic transfer that has been previously reported [15,36,37].

Additionally, we used the R-O-CDs to analyze Fe3+ in tap water, river water (Weihe River, Xinxiang City) and lake water (Muye Lake, Xinxiang City). The real water samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min following by being filtered through a 0.22 mm microporous membrane. The concentrations of Fe3+ in real water were also measured by using atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) method. As shown in Table 1 , it clearly revealed that the concentration of Fe3+ obtained by our fluorescence method were in good agreement with the values obtained by AAS, which indicated that the R-O-CDs sensing system for analyzing Fe3+ was quite reliable.

The detection mechanism for PPi is illustrated in Fig. 3e. Firstly, Fe3+ bound to the carboxyl and organic nitrogen groups on the surface of R-O-CDs to form a R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex and quenched the fluorescence of the R-O-CDs through the PET mechanism. The addition of PPi to the R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex system recovered the fluorescence of the R-O-CDs, which was resulted that the coordination bond of Fe3+ for PPi was stronger than the interaction between Fe3+ and the R-O-CDs [38]. Fig. 4f shows that the zeta potential of R-O-CDs shifted from −12.7 mV to 6.64 mV after addition of Fe3+. Then, the value returned to −10.2 mV after further in the presence of PPi, which supported that Fe3+ initially coordinated with the carboxyl and organic nitrogen groups of R-O-CDs and then detached from R-O-CDs to bind with PPi upon its introduction [32]. To further verify the mechanism of the PPi detection strategy, the fluorescence emission spectra of the R-O-CDs were measured under various conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 a, R-O-CDs shows strong fluorescence emission (curve a). However, the fluorescence of the R-O-CDs was quenched with addition of 150 μM of Fe3+ (curve b), Subsequent addition of PPi effectively recovered the fluorescence intensity of R-O-CDs/Fe3+ (curve c). Moreover, adding only PPi to the R-O-CD solution had no effect on the fluorescence emission of the R-O-CDs (curve d). These results indicated that the proposed detection strategy can be used to detect PPi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the sensing system for PPi detection
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure that the R-O-CDs/Fe3+platform could be used for the quantitative detection of PPi, the fluorescence response induced by PPi at different concentrations was evaluated. Fig. 5b shows that the fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex system gradually recovered as the PPi concentration was increased over the range of 0–225 μM. The relationship between the PPi concentration and F/F0 is shown in Fig. 5c, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex system in the absence and presence of PPi, respectively. There was a good linear relationship between F/F0 and the PPi concentration over a wide range (0–125 μM) (see the inset in Fig. 5c). The correlation equation was (F-F0)/F0 = 0.0058 × [PPi] (μM) + 0.9935 (R2 = 0.9932). The detection limit (LOD) was estimated to be 0.24 μM (S/N = 3). The change in the fluorescence intensity with time was monitored to analyze the kinetics of the PPi detection system. Fig. S12 shows that PPi incubated with the R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex system for approximately 3 min before the reaction reached equilibrium. Table S5 is a list of different systems and methods for PPi detection that have been reported in the literature. Our sensing system had a significantly lower detection limit than these systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the sensing system for PPi detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex system for PPi detection, the detection level for other biorelated active substances (such as metal ions, reductants, amino acids, etc.) was measured under the same experimental conditions. Fig. 5d shows that the fluorescence of the system was significantly enhanced only after the addition of PPi, and the other biologically relevant reactive species showed only a slight fluorescence response by themselves. These results indicated that the R-O-CDs/Fe3+ complex system had good selectivity for PPi and could be used to detect PPi in actual samples or biological environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of PPi in synovial fluids
ce:section.ce:para: The PPi concentration in the six synovial fluid samples was determined to evaluate the practicability of our using our designed sensor for real samples. The PPi concentration in the six synovial fluid samples was detected using both our designed sensor and a pyrophosphate assay kit (Sigma-Aldrich Co., ltd. Shanghai, China). Table 2 shows that the detection results obtained using our designed sensor were in good agreement with those obtained using the pyrophosphate assay kit. These detection results for PPi in synovial fluids confirmed that the designed sensing approach could be used for the detection of PPi in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a fluorescent IMPLICATION logic gate
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe3+/PPi-induced fluorescence switching in the R-O-CD system was exploited to design an IMPLICATION logic gate using Fe3+ and PPi as inputs, and the fluorescence intensity of the R-O-CDs as the output [39]. For the inputs, we defined the absence of Fe3+ or PPi to be the “0″ state and the presence of Fe3+ or PPi as the “1” state. For the output, the quenched fluorescence of the R-O-CDs was defined as the “0” state (off state), and the normal fluorescence of R-O-CDs was considered the “1” state (on state). Fig. 6 a (a truth table) and Fig. 6b (electric circuit schemes) show the four input combinations of (0, 0), (1, 0), (0, 1), and (1, 1). With Fe3+ as the sole input (1, 0), the fluorescence intensity (FL) of the R-O-CDs was quenched, and the output of the logic gate was “0” (the off state). In all other cases, the outputs of the logic gate were “1” (the on state) (Fig. 6c). The reversibility of this logic gate operation was also tested. Fig. 6d shows that alternating the addition of Fe3+ and PPi to the R-O-CD solution resulted in excellent repeated switching of the logic gate. The slight decrease in the output signal after several cycles was a dilution effect from the addition of Fe3+ and PPi.

In summary, red emissive carbon dots with tunable functional groups were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. The prepared R-O-CDs offer many advantages, such as facile preparation, nontoxicity, good water solubility and light stability. Then, an “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor has been developed for dual-detection of Fe3+ and PPi. A detection limit of 0.527 μM for Fe3+ and 0.24 μM for PPi were achieved. This method has good analytical performance, a wide detection range and a low detection limit. The successful detection of PPi in actual samples indicated that this sensor system has considerable application potential. Finally, an IMPLICATION logic gate was designed based on the sensor system to mimic the experimental results. The sensing platform developed in this study has the characteristics of simple operation, rapid reaction and high selectivity for Fe3+ and PPi detection and is a promising practical tool for biochemical applications.

Xiang Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Xiu Yan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Conghui Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yunxiao Ma: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yi Jiang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Rongjing Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Dongyang Shi: Formal analysis. Zhongjian Li: Methodology, Resources. Guifen Zhu: Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Bing Tan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705034 and 22006033), the Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in University of Henan Province (No. 20IRTSTHN011), the Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province (No. 202102210253), Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Henan Normal University (No. 2020JQ05), and Excellent Science and Technology Innovation Team of Henan Normal University (No. 2021TD06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108123.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
